Controller Area
Network
Frames & Frame Types
Module 3 of 6 —CAN Training Series

2
F R A M E A N AT O M Y
The CAN Data Frame (Standard, 11-bit)
2
A standard data frame is a fixed sequence of fields read left to right across the bus.
SOF
1 bit
Identifier
11 bits
RTR
1 bit
IDE / r0
2 bits
DLC
4 bits
Data
0–8 bytes
CRC
15 bits
ACK
2 bits
EOF
7 bits
Framing Arbitration Control Data CRC & ACK
Field
Purpose
SOF
Start
-of-frame dominant bit; synchronises all receivers.
Identifier
11
-bit message ID; also sets priority during arbitration.
RTR
Distinguishes a data frame from a remote (request) frame.
Control (IDE, r0, DLC)
Flags 11
- vs 29-bit ID and states the data length (0–8 bytes).
Data
The actual payload, 0 to 8 bytes.
CRC
15
-bit checksum plus delimiter for error detection.
ACK
Receivers assert a dominant bit to confirm correct reception.
EOF
Seven recessive bits mark the end of the frame.
F R A M E E X A M P L E
Standard Data Frame - Worked Example
3
A standard 11-bit data frame carrying identifier 0x123 with a 2-byte payload, field by field.
SOF
0
Identifier
0x123
RTR
0
IDE/r0
00
DLC
0010
Data
AB CD
CRC
15-bit
ACK
0
EOF
1111111
Transmit end (TX): Node loads ID 0x123, DLC=2 and bytes AB CD,
sends the dominant SOF, then streams each field MSB-first while monitoring the
bus.
Reception end (RX): Receivers sync on SOF, latch ID 0x123, read
DLC=2, capture AB CD, verify the 15-bit CRC, then drive the dominant ACK slot.
Bus timing — who drives each field
SOF Identifier RTR IDE/r0 DLC Data CRC ACK EOF
SENDER (TX) — transmits the entire frame
SOF · Identifier · RTR · IDE/r0 · DLC · Data · CRC released EOF
RECEIVER (RX) — samples all bits, asserts only the ACK
Listens to every bit and validates the 15-bit CRC ACK = 0
Bit transmission order → Blue = fields driven by the sender Orange = ACK bit driven by the receiver
I D F O R M AT S
Standard vs. Extended Frames (2.0A / 2.0B)
4
Aspect
Standard
— CAN 2.0A
Extended
— CAN 2.0B
Identifier length
11 bits
29 bits (11 + 18)
Unique identifiers
2,048
~537 million
IDE bit
Dominant (0)
Recessive (1)
Extra fields
—
SRR + IDE + 18
-bit extension
Typical use
General automotive & control
J1939, NMEA 2000, large networks
Interoperability
Coexists on the same bus
Coexists on the same bus
Both formats use identical arbitration and error rules — the extended ID simply appends an 18-bit field, letting standard and extended
frames share one physical bus.
M E S S A G E T Y P E S
The Four CAN Frame Types
5
Data Frame
Carries payload from a transmitter to all listeners; the workhorse
of the bus.
Remote Frame
Requests data from another node (RTR recessive, no payload).
Rarely used in modern designs and absent from CAN FD.
Error Frame
Any node that detects a violation transmits six dominant bits to
destroy the bad frame.
Overload Frame
Buys extra time by injecting delay between frames when a node is
not yet ready.
In practice automotive networks rely almost entirely on data frames; remote frames are largely deprecated and event/cyclic signalling is
handled at the application layer.
F R A M E A N AT O M Y
The Extended Data Frame (CAN 2.0B, 29-bit)
6
The 29-bit identifier splits into two parts, with SRR and IDE flagging the extended format.
SOF
1 bit
Identifier A
11 bits
SRR
1 bit
IDE
1 bit
Identifier B
18 bits
RTR
1 bit
r1 / r0
2 bits
DLC
4 bits
Data
0–8 bytes
CRC
15 bits
ACK
2 bits
EOF
7 bits
Framing Arbitration Control Data CRC & ACK
Field
Purpose
SOF
Start
-of-frame dominant bit that synchronises receivers.
Identifier A
First 11 bits of the 29
-bit ID; drives arbitration.
SRR + IDE
Recessive bits that mark the 29
-bit extended format.
Identifier B
The remaining 18 bits, extending the ID to 29 bits.
RTR
Distinguishes a data frame from a remote request.
Control (r1, r0, DLC)
Reserved bits plus the 0
–8 byte length code.
Data
The actual payload, 0 to 8 bytes.
CRC / ACK / EOF
Checksum, acknowledgement slot and end
-of-frame.
F R A M E E X A M P L E
Extended Data Frame - Worked Example
7
A 29-bit frame splits the identifier into an 11-bit base and 18-bit extension; SRR and IDE are recessive.
SOF
0
Base ID
0x123
SRR
1
IDE
1
Ext ID
0x1E6A7
RTR
0
DLC
0001
Data
5A
CRC
15-bit
Transmit end (TX)
Node sends 11-bit base 0x123, drives SRR and
IDE recessive to flag 29-bit format, then the
18-bit extension 0x1E6A7 and one byte 5A.
Reception end (RX)
Receivers see IDE=1, assemble the full 29-bit
ID 0x48DE6A7 from base and extension, and
accept the single data byte 5A.
Bus timing — who drives each field
SOF Base ID SRR IDE Ext ID RTR DLC Data CRC ACK EOF
SENDER (TX) — transmits the whole 29-bit frame
SOF · Base ID · SRR · IDE · Ext ID · RTR · DLC · Data · CRC released EOF
RECEIVER (RX) — samples all bits, asserts only the ACK
Assembles the 29-bit ID and validates the 15-bit CRC ACK = 0
Bit transmission order → Blue = fields driven by the sender Orange = ACK bit driven by the receiver
F R A M E A N AT O M Y
The Remote Frame (Data Request)
8
Same field layout as a data frame, but RTR is recessive and no data bytes are sent.
SOF
1 bit
Identifier
11 bits
RTR
1 bit
IDE / r0
2 bits
DLC
4 bits
CRC
15 bits
ACK
2 bits
EOF
7 bits
Framing Arbitration Control CRC & ACK
Field
Purpose
SOF
Start
-of-frame dominant bit; synchronises all receivers.
Identifier
11
-bit message ID; also sets priority during arbitration.
RTR
Recessive here
— this is what marks the frame as a remote request.
Control (IDE, r0, DLC)
Flags 11
- vs 29-bit ID; DLC states the requested data length.
No data field
A remote frame carries no payload
— it only asks for data.
CRC
15
-bit checksum plus delimiter for error detection.
ACK
Receivers assert a dominant bit to confirm correct reception.
EOF
Seven recessive bits mark the end of the frame.
F R A M E E X A M P L E
Remote Frame - Worked Example
9
A remote frame requests identifier 0x200 with no data; RTR is recessive and DLC states the expected length.
SOF
0
Identifier
0x200
RTR
1
IDE/r0
00
DLC
0010
Data
none
CRC
15-bit
ACK
0
EOF
1111111
Transmit end (TX)
Requesting node sends ID 0x200 with RTR=1
and DLC=2, but omits the data field
entirely - it is asking, not sending.
Reception end (RX)
The node that owns 0x200 recognises the
request and answers with a matching data
frame carrying two bytes of payload.
Bus timing — who drives each field
SOF Identifier RTR IDE/r0 DLC Data CRC ACK EOF
SENDER (TX) — requesting node transmits the frame
SOF · Identifier · RTR · IDE/r0 · DLC · Data · CRC released EOF
RECEIVER (RX) — the data owner asserts only the ACK
Owner of 0x200 checks the request and validates the CRC ACK = 0
Bit transmission order → Blue = fields driven by the sender Orange = ACK bit driven by the receiver
REQUEST & RESPONSE
Remote Request → Data Response
10
Node A asks for message 0x200 with a remote frame (RTR = 1, no data); Node B, the owner of 0x200, answers with a data frame carrying AB CD.
1 Node A — Requester
Sends a remote frame: RTR = 1, DLC = 2, no payload — requesting message 0x200 from
whoever owns it.
2 Node B — Data owner
Recognises 0x200, then replies with a data frame: RTR = 0, DLC = 2, data AB CD.
Bus timing — who drives each field
Phase 1 · Remote frame — Node A transmits Phase 2 · Data frame — Node B transmits
SOF ID RTR Ctl DLC —CRC ACK EOF SOF ID RTR Ctl DLC Data CRC ACK EOF
Node A
Node B
SOF · ID · RTR · Ctl · DLC · CRC rel. EOF Monitors, then acks ACK
Monitors, then acks ACK SOF · ID · RTR · Ctl · DLC · Data · CRC rel. EOF
Node A drives the remote-frame body (SOF · ID · RTR · control · DLC · CRC · EOF) and asserts the ACK bit of Node B's data frame.
Node B drives the data-frame body (SOF · ID · RTR · control · DLC · data · CRC · EOF) and asserts the ACK bit of the remote frame.
Blue = node transmits the field Orange = node only asserts the ACK bit
F R A M E A N AT O M Y
The Error Frame
11
Any node that detects a violation transmits an error flag to destroy the faulty frame.
Error Flag
6 dominant bits
Flag Superposition
0–6 bits
Error Delimiter
8 recessive bits
Error Flag Error Delimiter
Field
Purpose
Error Flag
Six dominant bits that deliberately break bit
-stuffing to signal the fault.
Active vs passive
Error
-active nodes send dominant bits; error-passive nodes send recessive.
Flag Superposition
Other nodes detect the error and add their own flags
— up to 12 bits total.
Error Delimiter
Eight recessive bits that close the error frame.
Recovery
The corrupted message is automatically retransmitted afterwards.
WHERE TH E ERROR FR AME S ITS IN TH E BIT STREAM
Faulty Frame
violation detected here
Error Frame
flag + delimiter
Interframe Space
intermission · 3 recessive bits
Bus Idle
recessive until the next frame
F R A M E E X A M P L E
Error Frame - Worked Example
12
On detecting a violation a node transmits six dominant bits, breaking bit-stuffing to alert every node at once.
Error flag
000000
Superposition
<= 6 bits
Delimiter
11111111
IFS
111
Transmit end (TX)
The detecting node overrides the bus with six
dominant bits, deliberately violating the
5-bit stuffing rule to force a global error.
Reception end (RX)
Other nodes see the stuffing error, discard
the frame, add their own error flags, then
wait out the 8-bit recessive delimiter.
Bus timing — who drives the error frame
Error flag Superposition Delimiter IFS
DETECTING NODE (TX) — forces six dominant bits
Error flag · Superposition delimiter + IFS — bus released (recessive)
OTHER NODES (RX) — add their own flags, then all release
See the violation, discard the frame, echo an error flag add flags
Time → Blue = the node that starts the flag Orange = other nodes join (superposition)
F R A M E A N AT O M Y
The Overload Frame
13
A node that needs more time injects an overload frame to delay the next transmission.
Overload Flag
6 dominant bits
Flag Superposition
0–6 bits
Overload Delimiter
8 recessive bits
Overload Flag Overload Delimiter
Field
Purpose
Overload Flag
Six dominant bits, identical in form to an error flag.
Flag Superposition
Other nodes echo the flag, extending it up to 12 bits.
Overload Delimiter
Eight recessive bits that close the overload frame.
When it is sent
A receiver needs more time, or a dominant bit appears during interframe space.
Effect
Injects delay between frames without incrementing the error counters.
WHERE TH E OVERLOAD FRAME S ITS IN T H E BIT S T R EAM
Preceding Frame
just completed
Overload Frame
flag + delimiter
Interframe Space
next frame delayed
Bus Idle
recessive until the next frame
F R A M E E X A M P L E
Overload Frame - Worked Example
14
A busy receiver injects an overload flag of six dominant bits to buy time before the next frame begins.
Overload flag
000000
Superposition
<= 6 bits
Delimiter
11111111
IFS
111
Transmit end (TX)
A receiver that needs a delay sends six
dominant bits during the intermission,
signalling that it is not yet ready.
Reception end (RX)
Nodes detect the overload flag and postpone
the next transmission until the 8-bit
recessive delimiter has completed.
Bus timing — who drives the overload frame
Overload flag Superposition Delimiter IFS
BUSY NODE (TX) — forces six dominant bits
Overload flag · Superposition delimiter + IFS — bus released (recessive)
OTHER NODES (RX) — see the flag, then all release
Detect the overload flag and postpone the next frame echo flag
Time → Blue = the node that starts the flag Orange = other nodes join (superposition)
R E F E R E N C E
Glossary & Key Acronyms
15
A quick-reference card for the terms used throughout this deck.
Term
Meaning
SOF / EOF
Start / End of Frame delimiters
DLC
Data Length Code (payload size)
RTR / IDE
Remote request / Identifier
-extension bits
CRC
Cyclic Redundancy Check field
ACK
Acknowledgement slot
TEC / REC
Transmit / Receive Error Counters
Term
Meaning
CSMA/CR
Carrier
-sense access with collision resolution
DBC
CAN signal database (messages & signals)
ISO
-TP
ISO 15765
-2 transport for long data
UDS
Unified Diagnostic Services
SecOC
Secure Onboard Communication
ECU
Electronic Control Unit (a node)
